data.title: Libya's AGOCO completes Hamada-Zawiya oil pipeline repairs
data.description:
Libya's Arabian Gulf Oil Company (AGOCO) has completed repairs to a crude oil pipeline leak along the 18-inch Hamada-Zawiya line, it said on Thursday, after an oil leak in late May.

The leak prompted full isolation of the pipeline to halt the flow of crude, followed by the suction of oil from the pipeline and its return to the Tahara field for repumping.

Zawiya, 40 km (25 miles) west of Tripoli, is home to Libya's biggest functioning refinery, with capacity of 120,000 barrels per day (bpd).

The refinery is connected to the country's 300,000 bpd Sharara oilfield.

data.description:

data.title: Tecom Group Halts Dubai Bourse Trading due to Board Meeting
data.description:
Tecom Group (DFM:TECOM) suspended trading on the Dubai Financial Market from 10:30 am local time on Thursday due to a board meeting.

The business district developer will resume trading once the meeting results are published, according to a same-day release.

data.title: Seatrium Awarded FSRU Conversion Project by Kinetics; Shares Up Nearly 1%
data.description:
Seatrium was awarded a floating storage regasification unit conversion contract from Kinetics, an energy transition initiative by floating power company, Karpowership, according to a company release on Thursday.

The project includes three LNG Carrier conversions with an option for a fourth project.

The project is expected to start during the third quarter of the year.

Shares of the company were up nearly 1% in recent trading.

data.title: Stonepeak-Plus Infrastructure Fund Insider Bought Shares Worth $1,999,627, According to a Recent SEC Filing
data.description:
Luke R Taylor, Director, on June 30, 2025, executed a purchase for 77,595 units in Stonepeak-Plus Infrastructure Fund (NONE) for $1,999,627. Following the Form 4 filing with the SEC, Taylor has control over a total of 77,595 Class X Limited Partnership units of the fund, with 77,595 units held directly.

SEC Filing:

www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/2045458/000114036125024690/xslF345X05/form4.xml

data.title: German Private Sector Inches into Positive Territory in June, Final Data Shows
data.description:
Germany's private sector emerged from contraction in June, albeit only slightly, as a rise in manufacturing production was partly offset by a modest drop in services activity.

The final HCOB Germany Composite PMI Output Index rose to a three-month high of 50.4 in June from 48.5 a month ago, Hamburg Commercial Bank and S&P Global said Thursday. The final reading matched the flash estimate.

For the service sector, the final PMI clocked in at a three-month high of 49.7, compared with the initial estimate of 49.4 and the previous month's 47.1.

data.title: France Services Sector Improves to 9-Month High
data.description:
The HCOB France Services PMI rose to 49.6 in June 2025 from 48.9 in the previous month, exceeding the initial estimate of 48.7.

While the latest figure marked the tenth consecutive month of contraction in the services sector, the decline was the joint-slowest in the current ten-month sequence—matching that seen in September 2024.

The fall in new orders moderated for the second consecutive month and was the least pronounced since January.

Meanwhile, employment continued to shrink, as several firms reported reductions in permanent staff.

On the inflation front, input cost pressures remained relatively muted, while output prices rose for the fourth time in the past five months.

Lastly, business confidence improved significantly in June, with service providers increasingly optimistic about the year ahead, as easing inflationary pressures and expectations of a more accommodative monetary policy are seen as key factors likely to support household spending and business investment.

data.title: Italy Services Activity Growth Slows
data.description:
The HCOB Italy Services PMI fell to 52.1 in June 2025 from May’s near 12-month high of 53.2, below market forecast of 52.7.

While it marked the seventh straight month of expansion, it was the weakest since March.

Output and new orders continued to expand at above-average rates, supported by new service launches and client wins, but the momentum softened.

Export sales dropped at the fastest pace in five months amid waning demand from key European markets and global trade tensions.

Input cost pressures intensified, driven by higher wages and rising energy and raw material prices.

Despite this, firms showed restraint in passing on costs, with selling prices rising at a softer rate.

On a brighter note, employment rose for a fifth month, with the rate of job creation the fastest in a year, leading to a further drop in backlogs.

Lastly, firms remained optimistic about activity over the next 12 months, buoyed by new client introductions and hopes for more stable market conditions.

data.title: HSBC, First Abu Dhabi to List Digital Bond on Abu Dhabi Securities Exchange
data.description:
HSBC Holdings teamed up with the Abu Dhabi Securities Exchange and First Abu Dhabi Bank to launch and list a digital bond.

The companies commenced the pricing stage as the initial step toward listing of the distributed ledger technology-based bond, which will be issued by First Abu Dhabi Bank using the digital assets platform HSBC Orion, according to a Thursday release. The digital bond would be the first in the Middle East and North Africa region.

British bank HSBC served as the sole bookrunner for the deal.

data.title: June Jobs Report: What Do Economists Expect? — WSJ
data.description:
By WSJ Staff

  • The U.S. likely added 110,000 jobs in June, according to economists polled by The Wall Street Journal.
  • That would mark a slight slowdown from May, when 139,000 positions were added.
  • The unemployment rate is seen rising slightly to 4.3%, up from 4.2% a month earlier.
  • The monthly nonfarm payrolls report, from the Labor Department's Bureau of Labor Statistics, is due at 8:30 a.m. ET.
  • On Wednesday, a separate report from ADP showed the private sector shedding jobs for the first time in more than two years.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Gold Futures Rise as Market Positions for Fresh U.S. Economic Data — Market Talk
data.description:
0804 GMT - Gold futures rise as investors await crucial U.S. economic data. Futures are up 0.2% at $3,354.90 a troy ounce. The precious metal has ticked higher ahead of U.S. nonfarm payroll data due later Thursday. The data will be closely analyzed for fresh signals on the economic outlook and the size and scope of potential interest-rate cuts from the Fed. Lower interest rates typically benefit non-interest bearing bullion. Gold has steadily recouped much of the losses incurred toward the end of June on persistent market uncertainty, as concerns are raised that President Trump's tax-and-spending megabill will add $3.3 trillion to the fiscal deficit. Trade uncertainty is also high as the 90-day reprieve on Trump's so-called reciprocal tariffs ends July 9, adding to gold's safe-haven appeal. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0801 GMT - Asia's reliance on trade makes the region vulnerable to tariff tensions, and many central banks have room to ease monetary policies as a buffer against the economic impact, Fidelity International says in a report. Growth prospects in the region are strong, supported by favorable structural shifts in China, Japan and India, alongside AI supply chains in South Korea and Taiwan, they say. Demand for the region's currencies is likely to pick up as investors seek alternatives to the U.S. dollar, says portfolio manager Terrence Pang. "There are a number of areas where Asia is set to benefit from the shift," with rate cuts and the potential for currency appreciation likely to boost the demand for the regional local currency bonds, Pang says. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

0801 GMT - Yields on U.K. government bonds, or gilts, fall, reversing Wednesday's sharp rise as markets worried about the U.K.'s fiscal position and the possibility of a new treasury chief. The U.K. Prime Minister later on Wednesday asserted that Rachel Reeves would remain Chancellor of the Exchequer in an interview with the BBC, easing market fears. The 10-year gilt yields fall 5 basis points to 4.534%, Tradeweb data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0759 GMT - The euro's strength is positive for the eurozone economy despite speculation that European Central Bank officials are growing concerned over the single currency's appreciation, ING's Chris Turner says in a note. Such concerns would be that a stronger euro lowers import prices and brings down inflation while making eurozone exports more expensive, he says. However, Europe should be taking advantage of this "global euro moment" as previously hailed by ECB President Christine Lagarde, the analyst says. Global portfolios re-allocated to the eurozone "can only be a good thing for private sector borrowing costs." The euro trades flat at $1.1801 after hitting a nearly four-year high of $1.1829 on Tuesday, according to LSEG. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0752 GMT - If Asean wants to produce the next tech giant, it will need a wider regional industrial policy, says Malaysia's deputy trade minister, Liew Chin Tong. Speaking at the 9th ASEAN Conference, he said that Asean governments have to start thinking about growing companies, including technology players, together. Except for Indonesia, the region's economies may be too small to pursue industrial advancements individually. Liew thinks the region will have to pool resources to build the next Huawei or TSMC. "If the governments of Asean are prepared to consider breaking from the past understanding of trade and industry... [and] pursue an Asean industrial policy, I think we will be able to see a different landscape and trajectory," he said. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0750 GMT - Shares in U.K. housebuilders rise in early trade after Bank of England Governor Andrew Bailey hinted at a possible cut in interest rates. Speaking at an European Central Bank's forum in Portugal, Bailey said he had observed some softening in the U.K. economy and labor market. Bailey noted that inflationary pressures, such as higher energy prices and average wages outpacing inflation, appeared to be cooling. Vistry shares rise 3.75% at 641.20 pence, Bellway climbs 2.8% at 2,684 pence, and Crest Nicholson trades up 1.9% at 184.10 pence. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

0751 GMT - Businesses must be prepared for exports to the U.S. to cost more for some time, says Singapore's Deputy Prime Minister Gan Kim Yong. "We cannot bank on the possibility that the tariffs will go away after four years with a different U.S. administration," Gan said at the 9th ASEAN Conference. There's growing bipartisan consensus in the U.S. on the use of tariffs to achieve fair and balanced trade with the world, and reshore or near-shore critical industries, he said. Businesses must also be ready for more scrutiny over their production and supply chains. "Like its tariffs, the U.S.'s economic security concerns will not go away anytime soon, especially over what it deems as critical industries." (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0728 GMT - The U.S.- Vietnam trade agreement shows China could be the focus of trade policies, economists at Capital Economics say in a research note. The U.S. plans to impose a 40% tariff on transshippped goods through Vietnam. "That will be seen as a provocation in Beijing, particularly if similar conditions are included in any other deals agreed over coming days," they say, as Chinese exporters often use Southeast Asian countries as a hub to repackage and export to the U.S. Countries that hope to reach trade deals with the U.S. are likely to be expected to curtail some trade with China, they say. Meanwhile, how electronics are seen in the negotiations is a critical aspect, as they account for a third of Vietnam's exports to the U.S. and are currently exempt from Trump's tariffs, the economists say. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0726 GMT - The U.K. government bond market is likely to face increased volatility due to high levels of debt, XTB research director Kathleen Brooks says in a note. "A theme is emerging, since 2022 we have seen more frequent spikes higher in U.K. [gilt] yields, which is a feature of the U.K. bond market since Covid," she says. Gilt yields rose sharply on Wednesday after U.K. Prime Minister Keir Starmer didn't confirm that the Treasury Chief Rachel Reeves would remain in her post while facing questions in parliament. "This [volatility] is likely to continue until debt levels come down and public-sector spending rates return to pre-covid norms." (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0716 GMT - Bitcoin rises after a rally in U.S. tech stocks and a trade agreement between the U.S. and Vietnam. Gains in major tech stocks lifted the S&P 500 and Nasdaq composite to fresh records on Wednesday. President Trump said the agreement with Vietnam will charge 20% tariffs on imports from the south-east Asian country, down from the 46% levy announced in April. The deal raises hopes the U.S. is about to announce multiple trade deals before the pause in reciprocal tariffs against trading partners ends on July 9, Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. Bitcoin rises 0.2% to $109,423 after hitting a three-week high of $109,813 late Wednesday, according to LSEG. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0715 GMT - Only 16% of market participants believe the Federal Reserve is completely independent, Deutsche Bank Research's Jim Reid says in a global financial market survey. One quarter of respondents think the political pressure will lead to lower rates, the head of global economics and thematic research says, referring to President Trump's repeated comments on monetary policy. Meanwhile, 58% believe it will have a marginal influence on policy, he says. The survey was compiled based on 420 responses from around the world and was conducted between June 30 and July 2. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0711 GMT - There has been an erosion of confidence in U.S. exceptionalism since the start of the year, but views are reasonably split going forward, Deutsche Bank Research's Jim Reid says. Confidence will resurface according to 44% of respondents, while 49% expect a slow erosion over the next few years, the head of global economics and thematic research says in the summary of a global financial market survey. Despite recent events, only 7% foresee a rapid erosion with significant outflows, the survey finds. The survey was compiled based on 420 responses from around the world and was conducted between June 30 and July 2. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Rebound in Germany’s Private Sector Confirmed: PMI
data.description:
The HCOB Germany Composite PMI was confirmed at 50.4 in June 2025, marking a return to growth in private sector activity after a contraction in May (48.5).

However, the expansion was only limited as an increase in manufacturing production was partly offset by a decline in services activity.

Meanwhile, new orders rose for the first time in over a year, albeit marginally, buoyed by stronger demand for manufactured goods.

Furthermore, firms grew increasingly optimistic about the year-ahead outlook, with confidence improving in both monitored sectors.

Still, employment continued falling, in line with the trend seen in each month since June last year.

A broad-based decrease in backlogs of work pointed to a lack of pressure on operating capacity.

On the price front, latest data showed inflationary pressures continuing to ease.

Both input costs and average prices charged for goods and services rose at the slowest rate since October last year.

data.title: Thailand's Industrial Estate Sector May See Pick Up in Land Pre-Sales — Market Talk
data.description: Thailand's industrial estate sector may enjoy stronger on-quarter momentum in land pre-sales in 2Q, UOB Kay Hian analysts say in a research report. This view is supported by optimistic guidance from Amata Corp. and WHA Corp., the analysts note. Interest from data centre operators has been increasing steadily, with Amata currently in talks with 4-5 high-potential customers and WHA presently in negotiations with around 20 possible clients. Among the two companies, Amata is UOB Kay Hian's preferred sector pick as WHA's earnings growth may moderate somewhat given its high earnings base in 1Q. The brokerage has a buy rating and a target price of THB25.00 on Amata's shares, which are 0.7% lower at THB14.70. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Nomura Affirms Indonesia's 2025 Headline Average Inflation Forecast at 1.6%
data.description:
Nomura kept its 2025 average headline consumer price index forecast for Indonesia at 1.6%, expecting inflation to pick up to 2.1% in the second half from 1.2% in the first.

Headline inflation rose to 1.9% annually in June, faster than 1.6% in May, driven by higher food prices and slightly above consensus. Core inflation, however, stayed unchanged at 2.4%.

The trade balance rebounded to $4.3 billion in May from $200 million in April as exports strengthened and imports declined.

Nomura also sticks to its view that Bank Indonesia will cut its policy rate by another 50 basis points in 2025 to 5%.

data.title: Baltic Classifieds Group's FY25 Profit, Revenue Grow
data.description:
Baltic Classifieds Group said Thursday its profit and revenue in fiscal 2025 rose year over year.

Profit attributable to owners for the 12 months ended April 30 was 44.8 million euros, compared with 32 million euros a year ago. EPS moved to 0.093 euro from 0.065 euro.

The online classifieds group's revenue was 82.8 million euros, compared with 72.1 million euros earlier.

Meanwhile, the board recommended a final dividend of 0.026 euro per share, payable on Oct. 17 to shareholders on record as of Sept. 12. It paid 0.021 euro per share a year ago. This takes the full-year dividend to 0.038 euro per share, up from 0.031 euro per share in the year-ago period.

Looking ahead, the company anticipates fiscal 2026 revenue to grow "close" to fiscal 2025.

data.title: Gold's Price Volatility May Temper Ahead — Market Talk
data.description: Gold's upside price volatility may temper over the next few quarters after a feverish 18-month rally, say strategists at State Street Investment Management. However, supportive factors, including ETF inflows, central bank purchases and a weakening U.S. dollar, still favor a bullish upside for the yellow metal into 2026. The strategists continue to assign an 80% probability for gold prices to be flat-to-higher over the next 6-9 months, and likely hit $4,000/oz in their 30%-weighted bull case scenario. A weaker U.S. dollar trend coupled with a potentially dovish Fed pivot in 2H could help gold attract higher allocation from the record $7 trillion parked in money market mutual funds, they add. Spot gold is up 0.1% at $3,360.72/oz. (monica.gupta@wsj.com)

data.title: Turkey inflation dips to 35% in June, a bit below forecast
data.description:
Turkish annual consumer price inflation dipped a bit more than expected to 35.05% in June, official data showed on Thursday.

Month-on-month, inflation was 1.37%, the Turkish Statistical Institute said, also below forecasts. In May, it also came in lower than expected at 1.53% monthly and 35.41% annually.

In a Reuters poll, the monthly inflation rate was expected to be 1.45% in June, with the annual rate seen at 35.2%.

The domestic producer price index rose 2.46% month-on-month in June for an annual rise of 24.45%, the data showed.

data.title: EQT: Will Support Europa Biosite's Next Phase of Growth
EQT: Buying It From From Adelis Equity
US Nonfarm Payrolls Seen at 110K in June
data.description:
US nonfarm payrolls are expected to have risen by 110K in June 2025, marking the smallest increase in four months and a slowdown from May’s gain of 139K. The unemployment rate is projected to edge up to 4.3%, the highest since October 2021, from 4.2%, while wage growth likely eased to 0.3% from 0.4%.

Hiring in the leisure and hospitality sector may have slowed, reflecting softer consumer spending on tourism and travel-related services.

In contrast, the healthcare sector likely remained a key driver of job growth.

Overall, the report is expected to point to a continued cooling in the labor market, as uncertainty surrounding tariffs, trade, and immigration policies leads many employers to adopt a more cautious hiring stance.

Nonetheless, the data would continue to underscore a fundamentally resilient labor market, with monthly payroll gains in the 100K-170K range, sufficient to keep pace with growth in the working-age population.

data.title: Oslo Bors - Trading Suspension On Crayon Group
data.description:
Crayon Group Holding ASA :

  • OSLO BØRS - TRADING SUSPENSION ON CRAYON GROUP HOLDING ASA
